title: On-Time Resolution and SLA Breach Rate for CSM Service Work
dataSources: Primary: `index=business` `sourcetype="snow:incident"` and/or `sourcetype="snow:sn_customerservice_case"` (CSM) ingested by **Splunk_TA_snow** modular inputs; auto-extracted fields on each snapshot include `sys_id`, `number`, `state`, `priority`, `parent_incident`, `sys_updated_on`, `sys_created_on`, `opened_at`, `resolved_at`, `closed_at`, `business_duration`, `calendar_duration`, `company`, `account`, `u_account`, `contact_type`, `assignment_group`, `category`, `subcategory`, `has_breached` when mapped from SLA rows. Complement: `index=business` `sourcetype="snow:task_sla"` with `task` (parent sys_id), `sla_due`, `has_breached`, `stage`, `start_time`, `breach_time`, `business_pause_duration` for Table API–accurate breach logic when incident-level breach flags are absent. Index may be `itsm` in your org—retarget `index=` consistently with the role’s `srchIndexesAllowed`.
app: `Splunk_TA_snow` — **Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow** ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)) on a Heavy Forwarder (modular `servicenow_record_input` Table API polling) and Search Heads (field transforms, CIM-optional), plus optional HEC from ServiceNow business rules; package name in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_snow`.
spl:
index=business (sourcetype="snow:incident" OR sourcetype="snow:sn_customerservice_case") earliest=-30d@d
| eval _time=strptime(sys_updated_on, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| dedup sys_id sortby -_time
| eval state_n=tonumber(state)
| where isnotnull(state_n) AND state_n IN (6,7) AND (isnull(parent_incident) OR parent_incident="" OR parent_incident="0")
| join type=left max=0 sys_id [
    search index=business sourcetype="snow:task_sla" earliest=-30d@d
    | stats latest(sla_due) as sla_due latest(has_breached) as has_breached_tsla latest(stage) as sla_stage by task
    | rename task as sys_id
  ]
| eval priority_n=tonumber(priority)
| eval priority_label=case(priority_n=1,"P1-Critical",priority_n=2,"P2-High",priority_n=3,"P3-Moderate",priority_n=4,"P4-Low",priority_n=5,"P5-Planning",1=1,"Unspecified")
| eval account_dim=coalesce(account, u_account, company, "Unassigned")
| eval resolved_epoch=strptime(coalesce(resolved_at, closed_at), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval mttr_business_s=tonumber(business_duration)
| eval mttr_wall_s=tonumber(calendar_duration)
| eval breached_flag=if(isnotnull(has_breached_tsla), if(tonumber(has_breached_tsla)=1 OR lower(tostring(has_breached_tsla)) IN ("true","t"),1,0), if(tonumber(has_breached)=1 OR lower(tostring(has_breached)) IN ("true","t"),1,0))
| stats count as ticket_count sum(breached_flag) as sla_breaches avg(mttr_business_s) as avg_mttr_business_s perc95(mttr_business_s) as p95_mttr_business_s by priority_label, account_dim
| eval sla_compliance_pct=if(ticket_count>0, round(100 * (1 - sla_breaches/ticket_count), 1), null())
| eval avg_mttr_business_h=round(avg_mttr_business_s/3600, 2)
| eval p95_mttr_business_h=round(p95_mttr_business_s/3600, 2)
| sort priority_label, account_dim
description: Rolled-up ServiceNow CSM and incident work shows which accounts and priority bands finish inside contracted resolution SLAs, how often **has_breached** fires on the linked **task_sla** rows, and the distribution of **business_duration**-based repair times after you strip child duplicates. Support and customer-success leadership use the view to reallocate people, press vendors on data quality, and brief executives before account reviews—without hand-exporting a ServiceNow report.
value: Missing contractual response or resolution windows directly affects renewal risk, penalty clauses, and B2B NPS. When this search reports breach rates above 5% for P1–P2 in a named account, capacity and escalation paths get funded with numbers tied to the same **business_duration** math your process owners defend in QBRs. Tightening the poll/alert loop here typically pays for itself the first time it prevents a late penalty letter or a forced service credit in a six- or seven-figure support contract.
implementation: (1) Install **Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow** (1928) and enable Table API inputs for `incident`, `task_sla`, and `sn_customerservice_case` (if licensed) to `index=business` with a **sys_updated_on** checkpoint; (2) confirm **business_duration** and breach fields are present (join **task_sla** when incident rows omit them); (3) save the main SPL as a scheduled report; (4) add an alert on **sla_compliance_pct** or **sla_breaches** by **priority_label**; (5) publish a Dashboard Studio board with the visualization mix in this UC; (6) add a throttled webhook to CS leadership when P1–P2 breach share spikes week over week.
visualization: (1) `splunk.column` of **sla_compliance_pct** by `priority_label` (stacked or clustered) to show the guardrail mix across severities. (2) `splunk.heatmap` of **account_dim** vs **priority_label** for **sla_breaches** counts to spotlight accounts that need a named response plan. (3) `splunk.singlevalue` with **sla_breaches** sum and a seven-day trend sparkline. (4) `splunk.table` with **account_dim**, **priority_label**, **ticket_count**, **sla_compliance_pct**, **p95_mttr_business_h**, and a drilldown to a simple XML or Studio **event** panel of underlying **number** values. (5) Optional `splunk.pie` of breach vs non-breach for an executive **donut** when filtered to one account.

references.title: Splunk Docs — Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (inputs and field mapping)
ServiceNow product docs — Task SLA engine
ServiceNow product docs — REST Table API (CRUD, sysparm_query, paging)
ServiceNow product docs — OAuth 2.0 for REST integrations
Splunk — Use the HTTP Event Collector (optional HEC from ServiceNow)

knownFalsePositives: A case moved from P4 to P1 mid-flight rebinds a new **task_sla** row and the breach flag on the earlier SLA definition can look like a false miss until you read **stage** and **start_time** on the new row. **Account** and **company** M2M fields sometimes fan out to multiple `account` values per ticket when territories overlap; **stats** without `mv` handling then inflates or splits row counts. When an incident reopens from Resolved to In Progress, the SLA engine can emit a fresh clock while Splunk still holds the first resolved snapshot—breach and compliance figures diverge for one poll until the **sys_updated_on** watermark catches the reopen. Parent/child CSM work where **child** rows still show Resolved will hide real backlog if you filter **parent** only; conversely, counting both parent and children doubles volume—your filter must match how your operating model prices SLAs. Finally, a **cmn_schedule** change (holidays, DST, or a new 24×7 **calendar_id**) rewrites **business_duration** for tickets straddling the change even though the wall-clock **calendar_duration** is unchanged.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• **Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow** (`Splunk_TA_snow`, Splunkbase 1928) 8.x+ on a Heavy Forwarder that can open **HTTPS/443** to your instance host `https://<instance>.service-now.com` and to your indexers/HEC, plus the same add-on (or a lightweight stub) on Search Heads for **props/transforms** that normalize fields on **snow:incident**, **snow:task_sla**, and other `snow:` family sourcetypes.
• ServiceNow: **ITSM/CSM** with Table API and OAuth 2.0 client for the integration user; rights to read `incident`, `task_sla`, `cmn_schedule` (if you validate business calendars in UI), and `sn_customerservice_case` when you route CSM work through the case table.
• Splunk: a business or ITSM index (here **`business`**) in **srchIndexesAllowed** for the CS-ops role. Plan license for poll volume: the Table API returns up to 200 rows per page by default, and the add-on re-queries using **sys_updated_on** so burst edits create bursts of events.
• Time semantics: the platform stores timestamps in **GMT/UTC**; the add-on’s `_time` is derived from **sys_updated_on** (or your chosen time field) without shifting to the user’s local zone, so a ServiceNow list report in a rep’s **America/Chicago** profile will not pixel-match a Splunk panel unless you **eval** with **strptime** and a named zone for display.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection**
The **`snow:incident`** sourcetype is the canonical sourcetype emitted by the **Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow** (Splunkbase 1928). Field extraction names below match the Add-on’s parsed output for Table API **incident** rows. CSM cases use **`snow:sn_customerservice_case`** with analogous lifecycle fields; include both in one dashboard by unioning in SPL or by splitting inputs.

On the collection tier, add or edit a modular input: **Settings → Data inputs → ServiceNow** (or `inputs.conf` under `Splunk_TA_snow/local`). Stanzas of type `servicenow_record_input` must name the **instance** URL, **OAuth** client/secret, **table** (for example `incident`, `task_sla`, `sn_customerservice_case`), a **sysparm_query** that keeps scope bounded (e.g. `u_source=enterprise` for a pilot), and a poll interval. The add-on tracks the last **sys_updated_on** per **(instance, table)** in a local checkpoint. Default polling is often 10 minutes—tighten for executive-facing SLA boards only after you check ServiceNow’s **Table API** throughput and the integration user’s transaction quota.

Breach and due dates live on **`task_sla`**: configure a second input (or a broader `sysparm_query` on a dedicated poll) for **`task_sla`** with `sysparm_fields` including **task**, **has_breached**, **sla_due**, **stage**, **business_pause_duration**, and **breach_time**. The REST path is the standard `.../api/now/table/{tableName}` form described in the ServiceNow **REST** reference. Page size, **sysparm_offset** pagination, and the **OAuth** access token (commonly 30 minutes TTL) drive steady-state catch-up; when backlogs grow, the forwarder’s poll duration per table rises linearly with row volume.

**Webhook / HEC path:** Some teams push high-touch tickets through a **Business Rule** that POSTs to Splunk HEC. Those events can land with **`sourcetype`=`snow:webhook`** (deployment-specific); normalize **sys_id** and time fields the same as Table API so the `join` in Step 2 still works.

**MID Server** is not the default for this add-on’s cloud REST poller, but on-prem allowlists and IP pinning sometimes force a MID-mediated route; if your enterprise requires MID, the ServiceNow side still materializes the same `task_sla` rows—only the network path to Splunk changes, not the field contract.

**Step 2 — Create the search and alert**
Use the `spl` field on this use case. Operationally, treat **dedup sys_id** as “latest image per ticket” after `sortby -_time` on **sys_updated_on**; resolved-to-reopen flips that matter for SLA are visible only after the new update lands, which can lag a human’s UI click by one poll. The **left** join to **`snow:task_sla`** rehydrates **has_breached** when the incident event omits a flattened copy.

For alerting, clone the search and add `where priority_label IN ("P1-Critical","P2-High") AND sla_compliance_pct < 90` (tune the threshold to the contract) after the final `stats`, or alert on a **weekly** `eventstats` of seven-day `sla_breaches` by **account_dim** to reduce noise. Schedule every four hours to align with the Table API’s batch nature; sub-hour runs only make sense with HEC or a five-minute **servicenow_record_input** interval. Throttle the alert on **account_dim** to four hours to avoid one noisy bank holiday weekend paging CS leadership continuously.

**Step 3 — Validate**
In ServiceNow, open **Service Desk → Incidents** (or **CSM → Cases**) and match five **number** values returned by a drilldown from the Splunk `table` panel. For each, confirm **State**, **Priority**, and **Parent** against Splunk. Then open the related **SLA** tab or **Task SLA** related list: **has_breached**, **Actual elapsed time** (business) vs the target, and the **Breach time** should align with the Splunk `breached_flag` and **mttr_business_s** for the final resolved row.

Compare **business_duration** to **calendar_duration** in the **Resolution information** where both appear; a Friday 16:00 open / Monday 09:00 resolve should show a small business hour span and a large wall span when the **SLA** uses a weekday calendar. If Splunk and the UI differ only by a zone offset, you are looking at a display-timezone effect, not an ingestion error.

Count-check: for one day, `| tstats count where index=business sourcetype="snow:incident" by _time` should have no long flat gaps; if gaps appear, the forwarder or OAuth token renewal failed (see Step 5).

**Step 4 — Operationalize**
Publish Dashboard Studio: row 1 — **singlevalue** for rolling 30d **sla_compliance_pct** (P1–P2 only via a `where` in the post-process). Row 2 — **column** chart of compliance by `priority_label`. Row 3 — **heatmap** of **account_dim** by week with **breached_flag** counts. Row 4 — saved **PDF**/Teams schedule for the VP of Customer Success. Tie drilldowns to the `number` field with a link template to `https://<instance>.service-now.com/incident.do?sys_id=$row.sys_id$` (replace with `sn_customerservice_case` for pure CSM).

Route alerts: P1–P2 **missed-target** to the CS war room; P3+ to a daily digest. Document that **calendar_id** and **cmn_schedule** changes are a normal root cause of week-over-week drift, not necessarily agent performance.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting**
• **Breach in UI but 0 in Splunk** — The **SLA engine** on the instance typically evaluates every 30 minutes; **has_breached** can flip in ServiceNow up to that window before a poll sees it. If you need fresher status, add a `where` in a fast search that compares `now()` to `strptime(sla_due,...)` for **stage** still **In progress**; treat that as a near-breach, not a legal breach, until `task_sla` confirms. Also verify you joined on **`task` = parent sys_id**; typos in `rename` make **has_breached_tsla** null and zero out **breached_flag** unless the incident carried its own field.

• **`business_duration` missing or null** — The duration fields populate only when **Resolved** and when the case used an SLA with a business schedule. Check **incident** vs **case** for field spelling; some CSM customizations store duration in a custom u_field—extend **FIELDALIAS** in **props** on the Search Head.

• **OAuth `invalid_client` or 401** from `oauth_token.do` — Rotate **client secret**, confirm the integration user is **not locked**, and reauthorize before old tokens expire. Access tokens (≈30 minutes) are short; the forwarder should refresh on 401, not log the same 401 for hours.

• **Table API 429/Rate limited** — Slow the **servicenow_record_input** interval, narrow **sysparm_query**, and split heavy tables across multiple stanzas with offset windows. ServiceNow throttles misbehaving clients per user; a dedicated service account keeps blast radius small.

• **Numbers disagree with a ServiceNow list filtered by the user’s time zone** — The platform’s stored fields are still UTC. Export the same list in **UTC** or add `eval display_time=strftime(_time,"%F %T %Z")` after you set `_time` from **sys_updated_on**; mismatch without this step is expected when comparing a Chicago analyst’s “Today” to Splunk’s UTC bucketing.

• **Duplicate or inflated ticket counts** — Parent/child: confirm `parent_incident` filtering matches policy. Reassignment storms that clone rows are rare, but a bad transform double-ingesting the same `sys_id` is not; use `dedup` as written and check `_indextime - _time` for replay spikes.

• **MID Server vs direct REST** — If policy routes REST through a MID, validate `curl` from the MID host to the instance **443** path used by the add-on; a proxy timeout there stalls **sys_updated_on** without raising Splunkd errors to the index tier.

• **SLA “breached” on reopen** — When a ticket leaves **Resolved** and returns to **In Progress**, new **task_sla** rows with fresh **start_time** are normal; a flat monthly compliance rate without reviewing **reopen** volume understates true customer pain—track **reopen** rates separately (sibling FCR/effort UCs) alongside this metric.
grandmaExplanation: We time how well support keeps its promises for each important customer—like checking whether every pizza arrived while it was still hot—so leaders can fix staffing or contracts when too many “late” outcomes pile up, before renewals and penalties become the story.
